text: Hey guys, I'm Mark Healy, professional waterman and GoPro athlete. Today I'm going to take you
through a new accessory by GoPro and also give you some tips on how to get really insane underwater
shots. Before I go diving, I always like to prep my cameras ahead of time, preferably the night
before. Really important, make sure the inside of the port's clean. Get those moisture absorbing
wafers in there. Make sure they're not in the hatch of the camera so it doesn't leak. You want
a really good seal when you're diving. Also, most of the time I go in the water, I'm going to put
one of these floaties on so you have a fighting chance of finding your camera should it get lost
in the water. New to the accessory line is the GoPro dive filters. They come in two different
colors to suit your needs. Both are rated from 15 to 70 feet. You can go deeper, so definitely
not going to hurt. Both made of glass, amazing optics. The difference is this red filter,
you're going to use this in more blue water. Like I dive a ton in Hawaii, this is my go-to filter
and it's really going to make those colors pop. The magenta filter, on the other hand,
is something that you're going to use in fresh water, perhaps lakes or diving a kelp bed in
California. It's just either fresh or kind of green water.
These are all tool-free. They pop on and off that easy. You got to make sure you put a little
tether on there so it doesn't get accidentally knocked off the water. You can either go into
the water with it off and put it on just to make sure that there's no air bubbles between the port
and the filter, or if you have too much gear and you're messing around and you just want it on,
you can go down in the water, but pop it off or either shake out the bubbles because there is a
little space. You want water in between the support and the support. You can either go in the water
or just filter.
As opposed to surfing where I'm kind of thinking of slowing it down in post, diving I'm just looking
for the best image quality I can get while underwater. So I'm going to bump the frame rate down.
So 1440 by 30 is probably the most all-around dive-friendly setting that I know of. If you're not
worried about low-light situations, it's just super bright coral reef, Bora Bora,
tons of light through water. I go to 2.7k at 30 frames per second. That gives you more of that
really crisp cinematic quality. All right guys, I'm GoPro athlete Mark Healy with your GoPro
athlete tips and tricks. Today we went over the dive filters and also a couple of modes that I
like to use while diving. Now get out there, get a mask on your head, have fun.
Why wouldn't you want a mask on when you want to get whenever you can?
Why would you want a mask on?
